# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between 2D-QSAR and 3D-QSAR with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | 2D-QSAR | 3D-QSAR |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | 2D graph (atoms, bonds) | 3D atomic coordinates |
| Descriptors | log P, σ, MR, topological indices | Steric/electrostatic fields on a 3D grid |
| Conformation | **Not considered** | **Explicitly required** |
| Example methods | Hansch, Free-Wilson | CoMFA, CoMSIA |
| Example | $\log(1/C) = a\pi + b\sigma + cE_s + d$ | CoMFA contour maps of DHFR inhibitors |

2D-QSAR ignores 3D shape information, whereas 3D-QSAR captures **steric and electrostatic fields** surrounding molecules, enabling **pharmacophore-based design** of drugs.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is rigid molecular docking? Mention one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rigid molecular docking** is a computational technique in which **both the ligand and receptor are treated as rigid bodies**, with only **6 degrees of freedom** (3 translational + 3 rotational) explored.

- **Advantage (1 mark):** **Computationally very fast**; suitable for screening millions of compounds (e.g., DOCK software, Glide SP).
- **Disadvantage (1 mark):** **Biologically unrealistic** because receptors undergo induced-fit conformational changes upon ligand binding (e.g., kinase activation loop motion).

**Example:** Initial pose prediction in **DOCK 6** for rigid protein–rigid ligand screening campaigns.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **Hansch 2D-QSAR approach** with the linear free energy equation. Discuss the significance of the parabolic model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CoMFA methodology** in detail, including the type of probe, fields computed, grid spacing, PLS regression, and validation parameters. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Hansch Premise:** Biological activity depends on **hydrophobic, electronic, and steric substituent effects**.

**Step 2 — Linear Equation:**

$$\log\left(\frac{1}{C}\right) = a\pi + b\sigma + cE_s + d$$

where **π** = hydrophobic substituent constant, **σ** = Hammett electronic constant, **Eₛ** = Taft steric constant, **d** = constant.

**Step 3 — Substituent Constants Definition:**

$$\pi_X = \log P_X - \log P_H$$

$$\sigma_X = \log\left(\frac{K_X}{K_H}\right)_{ionization}$$

**Step 4 — Significance:** Each coefficient (**a, b, c**) gives the **sensitivity of activity** to that parameter. A positive **a** means hydrophobicity favors activity.

**Step 5 — Parabolic Model:** For very lipophilic compounds, activity may *decrease* beyond an optimal log P (receptor solubility issues):

$$\log\left(\frac{1}{C}\right) = a(\log P)^2 + b(\log P) + c\sigma + dE_s + e$$

The optimal log P is found by **d/d(logP) = 0**, giving **log P_opt = −b/(2a)**.

**Step 6 — Example:** Classical application in **substituted phenoxyacetic acids** as plant growth regulators.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — CoMFA Definition:** Comparative Molecular Field Analysis is a **3D-QSAR technique** introduced by Cramer et al. (1988).

**Step 2 — Workflow:**
1. Build 3D structures of aligned molecules.
2. Generate a **3D lattice** with default spacing **2 Å**.
3. Place an **sp³ carbon probe with +1 charge** at each grid point.
4. Compute **steric** (Lennard-Jones 6-12) and **electrostatic** (Coulomb) interaction energies.
5. Truncate energies exceeding **±30 kcal/mol**.
6. Build **X matrix** (N molecules × M grid points) and **y vector** (activity).
7. Perform **PLS regression** with cross-validation.

**Step 3 — PLS Validation:** Compute **q² (LOO cross-validated r²)**, **r²**, **SEE**, and **F-statistic**. The standard acceptance criterion is **q² > 0.5**.

**Step 4 — Contour Map Interpretation:**
- **Green** = bulky substituents favorable.
- **Yellow** = bulky substituents unfavorable.
- **Blue** = electropositive groups favorable.
- **Red** = electronegative groups favorable.

**Step 5 — Example Application:** CoMFA on **HIV-1 protease inhibitors** identified key steric and electrostatic regions for activity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **CoMFA and CoMSIA** in detail. Why is CoMSIA considered superior? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Flex-X, FlexS, AutoDock, and DOCK** software with their **advantages, disadvantages, and successful examples**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | CoMFA | CoMSIA |
|---|---|---|
| Probe atom | sp³ C, +1 charge | Property-weighted probe |
| Energy function | Lennard-Jones + Coulomb | Gaussian-type |
| Number of fields | 2 (steric, electrostatic) | **5** (steric, electrostatic, hydrophobic, HBD, HBA) |
| Cutoff needed | Yes (±30 kcal/mol) | **No** (smooth Gaussian) |
| Singularity issues | Yes, near atoms | **None** |
| Default lattice | 2 Å | 1–2 Å |
| Contour richness | Limited | **Pharmacophoric** |

**Why CoMSIA is Superior (3 marks):**
1. **Smooth Gaussian** eliminates numerical singularities.
2. **Five physicochemical fields** provide richer pharmacophore information (hydrophobic, donor, acceptor).
3. **Easier to interpret** contour maps with direct chemical meaning.
4. **Better alignment sensitivity** and more reproducible results.

**Example:** CoMSIA study on **cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) inhibitors** yielded q² = 0.72 vs. CoMFA's q² = 0.61.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Software Comparison (4 marks for table, 3 marks for examples):**

| Software | Algorithm | Advantages | Disadvantages | Successful Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flex-X** | Incremental construction (fragment-based) | Fast; FBDD-friendly; pharmacophore-type constraints | Limited receptor flexibility; no full entropy | Docking of **Nelfinavir** analogs into HIV protease |
| **FlexS** | Similarity-based alignment + incremental build | Excellent for **scaffold hopping**; handles full ligand flexibility | Requires reference ligand; slower | Novel **thrombin inhibitors** designed by scaffold hopping |
| **AutoDock** | Lamarckian GA + Monte Carlo + Local search | Open-source; grid-based; handles ligand flexibility | Limited receptor flexibility; sensitive to grid center | **HIV-1 integrase** inhibitor screening; **Gleevec** binding mode prediction |
| **DOCK** | Anchor-and-grow / shape complementarity (sphere matching) | Very fast; ideal for ultra-large VS (billion-scale) | Mostly rigid ligand; needs careful sphere generation | Used in **p53-MDM2** inhibitor discovery; **SARS-CoV-2 main protease** inhibitor VS |

**Detailed Advantages (1 mark each):**
1. **Flex-X** — fragments are docked incrementally; allows pharmacophore constraints.
2. **FlexS** — uses similarity to a known reference pose; great for lead hopping.
3. **AutoDock** — Lamarckian genetic algorithm combines global + local search.
4. **DOCK** — uses **sphere matching** (Kuntz algorithm) for very fast rigid screening.

**Disadvantages (1 mark each):**
1. **Flex-X** — limited flexibility for the receptor.
2. **FlexS** — requires a known reference.
3. **AutoDock** — accuracy dependent on grid spacing; scoring function approximate.
4. **DOCK** — rigid ligand assumption; misses induced-fit effects.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Examiner Feedback):**
> 1. **Confusing CoMFA & CoMSIA probe atoms:** CoMFA uses sp³ carbon with +1 charge; do not write "sp²" or "neutral". (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Missing q² > 0.5 threshold:** Always mention the cross-validation criterion for QSAR models. (Lose 0.5–1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting DOF in docking:** Rigid docking = **6 DOF**, not "3". (Lose 0.5 mark)
> 4. **MC vs MD confusion:** MC uses **Metropolis criterion** and does NOT compute forces; MD **integrates Newton's equations**. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Time step confusion:** Standard MD time step = **2 fs (with SHAKE)** or **1 fs (without)**. (Lose 0.5 mark)
> 6. **Hansch constants:** Do not confuse **π** (hydrophobic substituent constant) with **log P**. π is substituent-specific; log P is whole molecule. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### QSAR Essentials
- ☐ 2D-QSAR uses **descriptors** (log P, σ, MR); 3D-QSAR uses **3D field grids**.
- ☐ Hansch equation: $\log(1/C) = a\pi + b\sigma + cE_s + d$.
- ☐ Parabolic model used when activity decreases at high log P.
- ☐ CoMFA: **2 fields** (steric + electrostatic), **LJ + Coulomb**, **±30 kcal/mol cutoff**, **sp³ C +1 probe**, **2 Å grid**.
- ☐ CoMSIA: **5 fields** (steric, electrostatic, hydrophobic, HBD, HBA), **Gaussian function**, **no cutoff**, **no singularities**.
- ☐ PLS regression used for both; **q² > 0.5** is the validation threshold.

### Docking Essentials
- ☐ **Rigid docking** = 6 DOF (3 trans + 3 rot).
- ☐ **Flexible docking** = ligand flexible / receptor flexible / both.
- ☐ **Manual docking** = expert-guided interactive graphics.
- ☐ **Flex-X** = incremental construction (FBDD-friendly).
- ☐ **FlexS** = similarity-based docking (scaffold hopping).
- ☐ **AutoDock** = Lamarckian GA + MC (open-source).
- ☐ **DOCK** = anchor-and-grow + sphere matching (rigid ligand).

### Dynamics Essentials
- ☐ **Monte Carlo (MC)** = stochastic, uses Metropolis acceptance, no forces, no time.
- ☐ **Molecular Dynamics (MD)** = deterministic, integrates Newton's laws, uses force field, gives trajectory.
- ☐ MD force field components: $U = U_{bond} + U_{angle} + U_{dihedral} + U_{LJ} + U_{Coulomb}$.
- ☐ MD time step = **2 fs with SHAKE** (or 1 fs without).
- ☐ Boltzmann constant **k_B = 0.001987 kcal/(mol·K)**.
- ☐ Drug-receptor dynamics includes **induced fit, water displacement, allosteric shifts**.
- ☐ MC is used in conformational search; MD is used in conformational sampling and binding refinement.
